reasoning:
The NFCIRISK index currently sits at -0.576 as of October 24, 2025. It has been steadily decreasing over the past year, reflecting increasingly loose financial conditions, but has completely flattened (-0.576 to -0.577) over the past five weeks.

The FRED statistical tool forecasts a 46% probability of an increase by early November. This slight downward bias in the immediate term is supported by the 100% market expectation of a Fed rate cut on October 28-29, which will maintain accommodative, loose financial conditions and delay any structural tightening. Therefore, a sub-50% probability is appropriate for the near-term.

However, as the forecast horizon extends, two factors dominate:
1. Increased variance (uncertainty): Over longer periods, macro noise naturally pulls probabilities toward 0.5.
2. The "Floor Effect": Financial stress indices have an asymmetric distribution. Since the index is currently at multi-year lows (nearing standard historical floors around -0.7 to -0.8), there is significantly more room for it to spike upward during sudden market volatility than there is for it to smoothly grind lower. A single bad macroeconomic report or shock could cause the risk subindex to jump, meaning the longer-term probability (3 months out to January 2026) inherently skews slightly higher than the short-term probability, crossing above 50% due to mean reversion.

Probabilities shift smoothly from 0.45 (1 week) to 0.48 (1 month) to 0.52 (3 months).
